text: This video presents a robotic redo-hepatectomy, more specifically a segment IV B resection, seven years after open right hemihepatectomy. The patient is a seventy-five years old male with a history of child acerosis.

This video presents a robotic redo-hepatectomy, more specifically a segment IV B resection, seven years after open right hemihepatectomy. The patient is a seventy-five years old male with a history of child acerosis. In two thousand fourteen he underwent an open total mesorectal excision with ileostomy for a rectal cancer.

In two thousand fourteen he underwent an open total mesorectal excision with ileostomy for a rectal cancer. There were five large synchronous colorectal liver metastases, for which he received four cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy Folfox, followed by open right hemohepatectomy with partial resection of sequent IV-A. Postoperatively, eight cycles of adjuvant chemotherapy Folfox were administered. He underwent a yastemi closure a few months later.

There were five large synchronous colorectal liver metastases, for which he received four cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy Folfox, followed by open right hemohepatectomy with partial resection of sequent IV-A. Postoperatively, eight cycles of adjuvant chemotherapy Folfox were administered. He underwent a yastemi closure a few months later.

At the beginning of the year, after routine oncologic follow-up, he was diagnosed with a solitary colorectal liver metastasis in segment IVb. On the PET scan and MRI, you may see the liver metastasis in segment IVb. The maximal diameter of the tumor was measured as thirty-seven millimeters. Since he had a solitary metachronous colorectal liver metastasis, the patient was planned for an upfront liver resection. A robotic redo-hepatectomy was the approach of choice.

At the beginning of the year, after routine oncologic follow-up, he was diagnosed with a solitary colorectal liver metastasis in segment IVb. On the PET scan and MRI, you may see the liver metastasis in segment IVb. The maximal diameter of the tumor was measured as thirty-seven millimeters. Since he had a solitary metachronous colorectal liver metastasis, the patient was planned for an upfront liver resection. A robotic redo-hepatectomy was the approach of choice.

As compared to laparoscopy, robotic liver surgery has the advantage of a large range of motion and provides multi-credible access with higher visibility and control in neurosurgical space, helping him operating more accurately and intuitively. According to the Southampton laparoscopic liver resection difficulty score, which is based on the five preoperative factors as shown on the slide, intraoperative risk was calculated to be fifty four point seven percent, which classified the surgery as extremely high risk.

The video shows the patient's abdomen with visible scars from previous surgeries, including a midline intraumbilical incision, a right subcostal scar, and a scar from an ileostomy. A large incisional hernia is observed at the lateral side of the subcostal scar. The patient is positioned supine with a twelve-degree anti-Trendelenburg tilt.

The video shows the patient's abdomen with visible scars from previous surgeries, including a midline intraumbilical incision, a right subcostal scar, and a scar from an ileostomy. A large incisional hernia is present at the lateral side of the subcostal scar. The patient is positioned supine with a twelve-degree anti-Trendelenburg tilt.
Four 8-mm robotic ports are positioned, including a supra-umbilical camera port and three additional ports. An air-sealed 12-mm port for the assistant is placed in the left iliac fossa.
Four 8-mm robotic ports are positioned, including a supra-umbilical camera port and three additional ports. An air-sealed 12-mm port for the assistant is placed in the left iliac fossa.

During the insertion of robotic ports, significant adhesion formation is observed, necessitating extensive adhesiolysis. The liver is carefully separated from the diaphragm using monopolar scissors and a bipolar sealing device. The dissection of segment IVb is particularly challenging due to its proximity to the previous transection plane from the right hemohepatectomy. Full exposure of the liver segment is eventually achieved.

During the insertion of robotic ports, significant adhesions are visible, requiring extensive adhesiolysis. The surgeon uses monopolar scissors and a bipolar sealing device to carefully separate the liver from the diaphragm. Segment IVb is particularly challenging to free due to its location at the previous transection plane from the right hemohepatectomy. Full exposure of the liver is eventually achieved.
The surgeon frees the liver hilum from adhesions to prepare for the Pringle maneuver. A Foley catheter is placed around the hepatoduodenal ligament using the Hohenlund technique for inflow control.
The surgeon frees the liver hilum from adhesions to facilitate the Pringle maneuver. A Foley catheter is placed around the hepatoduodenal ligament using the Hohenlund technique for inflow control.
The surgeon performs intraoperative ultrasound to accurately identify the tumor, visible on the lower screen. The transection line is marked on the liver surface using monopolar scissors.
The surgeon performs intraoperative ultrasound to accurately identify the tumor, visible on the lower screen. The transection line is marked on the liver surface using monopolar scissors.

The surgeon places a traction suture using a resorbable multifilament suture and Hemolog to demarcate the lesion. The Pringle maneuver is performed by applying traction on the folic catheter and securing it with a boulder clamp for effective inflow control.

The surgeon places a traction suture using a resorbable multifilament suture and Hemolog to demarcate the lesion. The Pringle maneuver is performed by applying traction on the folic catheter and securing it with a boulder clamp for effective inflow control.

The surgeon uses a robotic sealing device to progressively resect the lesion, sealing and cutting tissue while performing caliclamping to identify portal pedicles. Vessels and bile ducts are clipped with Hemolog clips and transected using a bipolar sealing device.

The surgeon uses a robotic sealing device to progressively resect the lesion, sealing and cutting tissue while performing caliclamping to identify portal pedicles. Vessels and bile ducts are clipped with Hemolog clips and transected using a bipolar sealing device.

The surgeon performs parenchymal transection until the tumor lesion is fully resected. Gauzes are placed under the transected liver parenchyma, applying gentle pressure and a sprinkle of water to check for bleeding and biliary leaks. The resected specimen is placed in an endocatch bag and left in the abdomen until the end of the surgery.

The surgeon performs parenchymal transection until the tumor lesion is fully resected. Gauzes are placed under the transected liver tissue, applying gentle pressure and a sprinkle of water to check for bleeding and biliary leaks. The resected specimen is placed in an endocatch bag and left in the abdomen until the end of the surgery.

The surgeon removes the gauze from the transected liver surface, revealing a spot of bile indicating biliary leakage. The site of leakage is inspected, and a resorbable, barred monofilament suture is placed to address the issue.

The surgeon removes the gauze from the transected liver surface, revealing a spot of bile. The liver surface is inspected to locate the site of biliary leakage. A resorbable, barbed monofilament suture is then placed to address the leakage.

The surgeon releases the Pringle maneuver by removing the bulldog clamp and loosening the Foley catheter. Hemostasis is checked, and Surgiflo Hemostatic Matrix is applied to the liver surface. With no residual bleeding observed, the Foley catheter is fully removed.

The surgeon releases the Pringle maneuver by removing the bulldog clamp and loosening the Foley catheter. A check for hemostasis is performed, and Surgiflo Hemostatic Matrix is applied to the liver surface. With no residual bleeding observed, the Foley catheter is fully removed.

The surgeon applies Surgicel Fibrillar Resorbable Hemostat to the surgical site to ensure hemostasis. Attention is then directed to repairing the incisional hernia using a primary closure technique with a barbed monofilament resorbable suture, avoiding mesh placement due to the liver surgery. A silicone drain is left in place, typically used only when there is a perioperative bile leak. The specimen is exteriorized by enlarging the system port incision in the left iliac fossa.

Operative time was hundred seventy minutes, blood loss was thirty cc and the Pringle maneuver was performed for eighteen minutes. Pathology confirmed an R-zero resection of the colorectal liver metastasis. The post-operative course was uneventful and the patient was discharged on day two post-operative.
